food_subgroup: Stir-fried mushroom dish with black pepper sauce

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (mushrooms and other fungi, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for black pepper abalone mushroom stir-fry.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Zi Char shared serving of black pepper abalone mushrooms is approximately 200 g, representing one shared plate for two to three diners alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mushrooms, stir-fried with sauce — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central (Oyster mushrooms, cooked) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD (stir-fried vegetable dish with sauce) components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for black pepper abalone mushroom stir-fry exists in HPB Foodo, IMR MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked oyster/abalone mushrooms (FDC ID 169403) with a black pepper sauce contribution (oil, soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, black pepper) modelled from IMR MyFCD stir-fried vegetable dish components. Abalone mushrooms (Pleurotus abalonus) are nutritionally very close to oyster mushrooms (Pleurotus ostreatus). Energy and macros reflect a cooked dish with approximately 8–10% added oil and sauce by weight.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms and non-starchy vegetables — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black pepper abalone mushroom stir-fry. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of mushrooms and non-starchy cooked vegetables as very low GI (GI range 10–20). The sauce contribution (oyster sauce, soy sauce) adds minimal glycemic load. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 7.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~480 mg/100 g; ~960 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a full 200 g serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 42% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~15) and low glycemic load per serving (~2.3), with high dietary fibre from abalone mushrooms supporting postprandial glucose control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black pepper abalone mushrooms are a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low GI, low carbohydrate content (7.5 g/100 g), and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g); however, sodium from the sauce should be monitored in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (6.2 g/100 g) from stir-frying oil, with elevated sodium from sauce ingredients posing risk for blood pressure management in CVD patients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While abalone mushrooms themselves are cholesterol-free and provide beneficial beta-glucan fibre, the added cooking oil and high-sodium sauces in this stir-fried preparation warrant portion control and sauce reduction for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Abalone Mushrooms (黑椒鲍鱼菇, Hei Jiao Bao Yu Gu) is a popular Zi Char and Malaysian Chinese stir-fried dish featuring abalone mushrooms (Pleurotus abalonus) cooked in a bold, aromatic black pepper sauce. The dish is prepared by stir-frying thick-sliced abalone mushrooms in a wok over high heat with garlic, onion, butter or cooking oil, and a sauce made from freshly cracked black pepper, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and sometimes a touch of sugar or sesame oil. The result is a dish with a firm, meaty, slightly chewy texture and a deeply savoury, peppery flavour profile with umami richness from the oyster sauce. Abalone mushrooms are nutritionally dense, providing dietary fibre (including beta-glucan), B vitamins (particularly riboflavin and niacin), potassium, and phosphorus, while being naturally low in calories and cholesterol-free. The stir-frying process adds fat and sodium from the sauce, raising the energy density to approximately 98 kcal per 100 g. The dish has a very low glycemic index (~15) and is suitable for individuals managing blood glucose, though the sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) requires attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is commonly served as a shared side dish in Zi Char restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia, typically alongside steamed white rice. The dish is halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. It is sometimes listed on menus as 'Abalonemu Shrooms' or 'Black Pepper Mushroom' in casual dining contexts.
common_user_queries: Is black pepper abalone mushroom healthy?
How many calories in black pepper mushroom stir-fry?
Is abalone mushroom dish suitable for diabetics?
What is Hei Jiao Bao Yu Gu?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for black pepper abalone mushroom stir-fry exists in HPB Foodo, IMR MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA cooked oyster mushroom data combined with a modelled black pepper sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy cooked mushrooms. Confidence is medium rather than low because abalone mushrooms are nutritionally very similar to oyster mushrooms (same genus Pleurotus), and the sauce composition is well-characterised from standard Zi Char cooking practice.
notes: 1. The food name 'Black Pepper Abalonemu Shrooms' in the input is a colloquial/menu variant; canonical English name normalised to 'Black Pepper Abalone Mushrooms'. 2. Nutrient values are estimated (source_match_type: estimated) as no exact dish entry exists in approved primary sources; values should be replaced with laboratory analysis or an exact approved-source match if one becomes available. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no trans fats expected in a plant-based stir-fry dish using standard cooking oil). 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — abalone mushrooms are plant-based and contain no dietary cholesterol; oyster sauce may contain trace amounts but is negligible at dish level). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — abalone mushrooms are a plant-based food and do not naturally contain vitamin B12). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked oyster mushroom data (FDC ID 169403) and are approximate. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical Zi Char preparation without pork; actual halal certification status of the specific restaurant (Marina Seafood House - Boat Quay) is not confirmed. 8. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' (GI ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 7.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.25, rounded to 2.3.
